scenes.title: Probability Terms
scenes.description: A guided tour of every probability term in the joint distribution — each one highlighted in the rectangle while the rest fade back.

steps.title: All Terms at a Glance
steps.description:
Every probability term lives somewhere in this rectangle.

- **Column width** = marginal $P(X)$
- **Row height within each column** = conditional $P(Y \mid X)$
- **Cell area** = joint $P(X, Y)$

Step through to highlight each term one at a time.

info.content:
### Probability Terms
Adjust sliders to reshape
the rectangle, then step
through each term.

steps.title: $P(A)$ — Prior
steps.description:
**P(A)** is the marginal probability of event $A$ — the **width of the left column**.

It represents your prior belief about $A$ before observing anything about $B$.

info.content:
### $P(A)$ — Prior
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
---
Left column width.
Belief in $A$ before
observing $B$.
---
$P(A) + P(\overline{A}) = 1$

steps.title: $P(\overline{A})$
steps.description:
**P(¬A)** is the probability of the complement — the **width of the right column**.

$P(A) + P(\overline{A}) = 1$

info.content:
### $P(\overline{A})$
$P(\overline{A}) = {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$
---
Right column width.
$P(A) + P(\overline{A}) = 1$
${{pA}} + {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}} = 1$ ✓

steps.title: $P(B)$ — Marginal / Evidence
steps.description:
**P(B)** is the total probability of event $B$ — the **area of the entire B-row**, summed across both columns.

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A})\,P(\overline{A})$$

This is the **law of total probability**. The B-row is L-shaped because $P(B \mid A) \neq P(B \mid \overline{A})$.

info.content:
### $P(B)$ — Evidence
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
Total area of the B-row.
$= P(B|A)P(A)+P(B|\overline{A})P(\overline{A})$
$= {{pBgA}}\times{{pA}} + {{pBgNA}}\times{{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$

steps.title: $P(\overline{B})$
steps.description:
**P(¬B)** is the probability of $B$ not occurring — the **area of the ¬B region** (upper part of each column).

$P(B) + P(\overline{B}) = 1$

info.content:
### $P(\overline{B})$
$P(\overline{B}) = {{toFixed(1-(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
Upper region area.
$P(B)+P(\overline{B})=1$
${{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}+{{toFixed(1-(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}=1$ ✓

steps.title: $P(A \cap B)$ — Joint
steps.description:
**P(A∩B)** is the joint probability of both $A$ and $B$ — the **area of the bottom-left cell**.

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

info.content:
### $P(A \cap B)$
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
---
Bottom-left cell area.
$= P(A) \cdot P(B|A)$
$= {{pA}} \times {{pBgA}}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$

steps.title: $P(\overline{A} \cap B)$ — Joint
steps.description:
**P(¬A∩B)** is the joint probability that $A$ does not occur but $B$ does — the **area of the bottom-right cell**.

$$P(\overline{A} \cap B) = P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(B \mid \overline{A})$$

info.content:
### $P(\overline{A} \cap B)$
$P(\overline{A} \cap B) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
Bottom-right cell area.
$= P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(B|\overline{A})$
$= {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}} \times {{pBgNA}}$
$= {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$

steps.title: $P(A \cap \overline{B})$ — Joint
steps.description:
**P(A∩¬B)** — $A$ occurs but $B$ does not — the **top-left cell**.

$$P(A \cap \overline{B}) = P(A) \cdot P(\overline{B} \mid A) = P(A) \cdot (1 - P(B \mid A))$$

info.content:
### $P(A \cap \overline{B})$
$P(A \cap \overline{B}) = {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA),3)}}$
---
Top-left cell area.
$= P(A) \cdot (1 - P(B|A))$
$= {{pA}} \times {{toFixed(1-pBgA,2)}}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA),3)}}$

steps.title: $P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B})$ — Joint
steps.description:
**P(¬A∩¬B)** — neither $A$ nor $B$ occurs — the **top-right cell**.

The four joints sum to 1:
$$P(A\cap B)+P(\overline{A}\cap B)+P(A\cap\overline{B})+P(\overline{A}\cap\overline{B})=1$$

info.content:
### $P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B})$
$P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B}) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*(1-pBgNA),3)}}$
---
Top-right cell area.
---
All four joints:
${{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} + {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
$+ {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA),3)}} + {{toFixed((1-pA)*(1-pBgNA),3)}}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA+pA*(1-pBgA)+(1-pA)*(1-pBgNA),2)}}$ ✓

steps.title: $P(B \mid A)$ — Likelihood
steps.description:
**P(B|A)** is the conditional probability of $B$ given $A$ — the **row height within the A column**.

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

Geometrically: zoom into the A-column (blue border). The B-portion occupies $P(B \mid A)$ of its height.

info.content:
### $P(B \mid A)$ — Likelihood
$P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
---
Row height in A-column.
$= \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{pA}} = {{pBgA}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA))<0.02 ? 'Equal to P(B) — independent' : pBgA > pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA ? 'B more likely when A occurs' : 'B less likely when A occurs'}}

steps.title: $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ — False positive rate
steps.description:
**P(B|¬A)** is the row height within the ¬A column.

$$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = \frac{P(\overline{A} \cap B)}{P(\overline{A})}$$

When $P(B \mid A) = P(B \mid \overline{A})$, $A$ and $B$ are **independent**.

info.content:
### $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ — False positive rate
$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = {{pBgNA}}$
---
Row height in $\overline{A}$-column.
$= \dfrac{P(\overline{A} \cap B)}{P(\overline{A})}$
$= {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}} / {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}} = {{pBgNA}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Equal to P(B|A) — independent' : abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.1 ? 'Nearly independent' : 'Dependent — step is visible'}}

steps.title: $P(A \mid B)$ — Posterior
steps.description:
**P(A|B)** is the posterior probability of $A$ given evidence $B$ — the **A-fraction of the B-row**.

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is Bayes' rule. Geometrically: zoom into the B-row — the A-cell occupies $P(A \mid B)$ of its width.

info.content:
### $P(A \mid B)$ — Posterior
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
A-fraction of B-row.
$= \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Independent: P(A|B) = P(A)' : pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA) > pA ? 'B raised belief in A' : 'B lowered belief in A'}}

steps.title: $P(A \mid \overline{B})$ — Posterior given $\overline{B}$
steps.description:
**P(A|¬B)** — how likely is $A$ given that $B$ did **not** occur?

$$P(A \mid \overline{B}) = \frac{P(A \cap \overline{B})}{P(\overline{B})}$$

If $B$ is a positive test, this is the probability of the condition given a **negative** result.

info.content:
### $P(A \mid \overline{B})$ — Posterior given $\overline{B}$
$P(A \mid \overline{B}) = {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA)/(pA*(1-pBgA)+(1-pA)*(1-pBgNA)),3)}}$
---
A-fraction of $\overline{B}$ region.
$= \dfrac{P(A \cap \overline{B})}{P(\overline{B})}$
$= {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA),3)}} / {{toFixed(pA*(1-pBgA)+(1-pA)*(1-pBgNA),3)}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Independent: P(A|~B) = P(A)' : pA*(1-pBgA)/(pA*(1-pBgA)+(1-pA)*(1-pBgNA)) > pA ? '~B raised belief in A' : '~B lowered belief in A'}}

scenes.markdown:
# Probability Terms

Every probability term you will ever encounter lives somewhere in this rectangle. Three sliders — $P(A)$, $P(B \mid A)$, $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ — define the entire joint distribution of two binary events.

## The Rectangle Geometry

| Geometry | Term |
|----------|------|
| Left column width | $P(A)$ |
| Right column width | $P(\overline{A}) = 1 - P(A)$ |
| Row height in A-column | $P(B \mid A)$ |
| Row height in ¬A-column | $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ |
| Bottom-left cell area | $P(A \cap B)$ |
| Bottom-right cell area | $P(\overline{A} \cap B)$ |
| Top-left cell area | $P(A \cap \overline{B})$ |
| Top-right cell area | $P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B})$ |

## Derived Terms

**Marginals** (sum across rows or columns):
$$P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A})\,P(\overline{A})$$

**Conditionals** (fraction within a row or column):
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \qquad P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

## Independence

When $P(B \mid A) = P(B \mid \overline{A})$, the step line is horizontal — $A$ and $B$ are **independent**. Every conditional collapses to the unconditional: $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$, $P(B \mid A) = P(B)$.

## All 12 Terms from 3 Numbers

$$P(A),\; P(B \mid A),\; P(B \mid \overline{A}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{all 12 terms}$$

## Naming Conventions

$P(B)$ goes by two names depending on context:

- **Marginal probability** — the mathematical term. $P(B)$ is obtained by *marginalizing* the joint distribution over $A$: summing $P(A \cap B)$ and $P(\overline{A} \cap B)$.
- **Evidence** — the Bayesian term. When $B$ is an observation, $P(B)$ is the total probability of that evidence occurring, used as the normalizing denominator in Bayes' rule.

Same quantity, two lenses. You will see both in the literature.
scenes.prompt:
You are a patient tutor helping a student explore every probability term in the joint distribution rectangle.

## The 12 Terms and Their Geometry
All terms are determined by just three numbers: P(A), P(B|A), P(B|¬A).

**Marginals** — widths or total row areas:
- P(A) = left column width
- P(¬A) = right column width = 1 - P(A)
- P(B) = total B-row area = P(B|A)·P(A) + P(B|¬A)·P(¬A)
- P(¬B) = 1 - P(B)

**Joints** — individual cell areas:
- P(A∩B) = bottom-left cell = P(A)·P(B|A)
- P(¬A∩B) = bottom-right cell = P(¬A)·P(B|¬A)
- P(A∩¬B) = top-left cell = P(A)·(1-P(B|A))
- P(¬A∩¬B) = top-right cell = P(¬A)·(1-P(B|¬A))

**Conditionals** — fraction within a column or row:
- P(B|A) = row height in A-column (slider directly)
- P(B|¬A) = row height in ¬A-column (slider directly)
- P(A|B) = A-fraction of B-row = P(A∩B)/P(B) — Bayes posterior
- P(A|¬B) = A-fraction of ¬B region = P(A∩¬B)/P(¬B)

## Independence Special Case
When P(B|A) = P(B|¬A): the step line is horizontal, P(B) = P(B|A), and all conditionals collapse: P(A|B) = P(A|¬B) = P(A), P(B|A) = P(B|¬A) = P(B).

## Teaching Approach
- Point to the highlighted region in the rectangle when explaining each term
- Emphasize that all 12 terms come from just 3 slider values
- Use the independence check to connect conditional and marginal probabilities
- Encourage the student to predict how a term changes before moving the slider

scenes.title: Conditional Probability
scenes.description: Build intuition for conditional probability from joint distributions and areas — the probability rectangle.

views.name: Side
views.description: See the Z-layers from the side

scenes.markdown:
# Conditional Probability

## The Probability Rectangle

Represent the joint distribution of two binary events as a **unit square** (area = 1):

- **Column width** = $P(X)$ — marginal probability of $X$
- **Row height within each column** = $P(Y \mid X)$ — conditional probability of $Y$ given $X$
- **Cell area** = $P(X) \times P(Y \mid X) = P(X, Y)$

## Independence Is a Straight Line

When $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**, $P(Y \mid X = 0) = P(Y \mid X = 1) = P(Y)$. The horizontal divider is the **same height in every column** — a straight line.

When **dependent**, the divider is **stepped**.

## Conditional Probability

Conditioning on $B$ means focusing on just the $B$-row:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$$

Geometrically: **zoom into the B-row**, stretching it to fill the full square.

## Key Formulas

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{A})$$

steps.title: The Probability Rectangle
steps.description:
The **unit square** represents all possible outcomes (total probability = 1). We split it into two **columns** — one for event $A$, one for $\overline{A}$ — where the **column width equals the probability**.

Adjust $P(A)$ to see the column split change. This is the first axis of the joint distribution.

info.content:
### Column widths = P(X)
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
$P(\overline{A}) = {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$
---
Column width encodes
marginal probability.
Area of left column = $P(A) = {{pA}}$
Area of right column = ${{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$

steps.title: The Joint Distribution
steps.description:
Now add event $B$ as a **row split within each column**. The height of the $B$-row in each column is the **conditional probability** $P(B \mid \text{column})$.

Crucially, $P(B \mid A)$ and $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ can be **different** — that's dependence. Each cell's area equals the joint probability $P(X, Y)$.

**Independence**: set both $P(B \mid A)$ and $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ to the same value — the horizontal divider becomes a straight line.

sliders.label: $P(B \mid A)$ — row height in A column

info.content:
### Joint Distribution
Cell area = $P(X,Y)$
---
$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$
$\quad = {{pA}} \times {{pBgA}} = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(\overline{A} \cap B) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
**Independent?**
$P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = {{pBgNA}}$
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Yes — step line is horizontal' : 'No — step line is off-horizontal'}}

steps.title: Independence Is a Straight Line
steps.description:
**Independence** means knowing $A$ tells you nothing about $B$: $P(B \mid A) = P(B \mid \overline{A})$.

The **bar chart to the right** shows P(B|A) and P(B|¬A) side by side. When they match, the step line in the rectangle is a straight horizontal line — that is independence.

**Dependence** creates mismatched bars and a step. Set both sliders to the same value to see the bars align and the step disappear.

info.content:
### Independence
$P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = {{pBgNA}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
Step height:
$|P(B \mid A) - P(B \mid \overline{A})| = {{toFixed(abs(pBgA-pBgNA),3)}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? '✓ Independent — straight line' : pBgA>pBgNA ? 'B more likely when A occurs' : 'B less likely when A occurs'}}

steps.title: Conditioning on B: Zoom In
steps.description:
**Conditioning on B** means restricting attention to just the $B$-row — the bottom strip. We zoom in on it, stretching it to fill the entire square.

In the zoomed view (elevated at z = 2.5), the $A$ column occupies exactly $P(A \mid B)$ of the width. The **column widths change** when you zoom in, because the B-row has different heights in each column.

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\text{area of } A \cap B}{\text{area of } B\text{-row}} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$$

Use the **Side** camera view to see the 3D projection clearly.

info.content:
### Conditioning on B
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
$P(\overline{A} \mid B) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
Note: $P(A)={{pA}}$ but $P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Same! A and B are independent.' : 'Different — B shifts the belief about A.'}}

steps.title: Conditioning on A: Zoom In
steps.description:
**Conditioning on A** means restricting attention to just the $A$-column — the left strip. We zoom it horizontally to fill the entire square.

In the zoomed view (elevated at z = 2.5), the $B$ row occupies exactly $P(B \mid A)$ of the height. Compare this to the previous slide where we zoomed the B-row.

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{\text{area of } A \cap B}{\text{area of } A\text{-column}} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(A)}$$

Use the **Side** camera view to see both zoom levels.

info.content:
### Conditioning on A
$P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(A)}$
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
---
$P(B \mid A) = {{toFixed(pBgA,3)}}$
$P(\overline{B} \mid A) = {{toFixed(1-pBgA,3)}}$
---
Note: $P(B)={{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$ but $P(B \mid A)={{pBgA}}$
{{abs(pBgA-(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA))<0.02 ? 'Same! A and B are independent.' : 'Different — A changes the probability of B.'}}

steps.title: Asymmetry: $P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$
steps.description:
You have now seen both zoomed views — conditioning on B and conditioning on A.

Same four cells, two different lenses. The intersection area $P(A \cap B)$ is shared, but the denominators differ: $P(B)$ vs $P(A)$. That's why $P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$ in general — same intersection, different normalization.

info.content:
### Asymmetry
Same intersection, different denominators:
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$

$P(B \mid A) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{pA}}$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pBgA,3)}}$
---
{{abs(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA)-pBgA)<0.01 ? 'Equal here — symmetric: P(A) ≈ P(B)' : 'Unequal — this is the usual case'}}

scenes.prompt:
You are teaching conditional probability using the correct area-based rectangle model.

## The Core Geometric Model (CRITICAL)
The rectangle divides into four cells:
- **Columns** = values of X (A vs ¬A). Column WIDTH = P(X) — marginal probability.
- **Rows within each column** = conditional distribution P(Y|X). Row HEIGHT in each column = P(Y|X).
- **Cell area** = P(X) × P(Y|X) = P(X,Y) — joint probability.
- The dividing line between rows is STEPPED (not horizontal) when dependent.
- Independence means straight horizontal line: P(B|A) = P(B|¬A).

## When Explaining Conditioning
- Conditioning on B = zoom into the B-row (bottom strip)
- After zooming: A-column occupies P(A|B) fraction, NOT P(A) fraction
- This is why P(A|B) ≠ P(A) when dependent

## Exploration Prompts
- 'Set P(B|A) = P(B|¬A) and notice the step disappears — that is what independence looks like'
- 'Can you find slider values where P(A|B) is actually less than P(A)?'

scenes.title: Bayes' Rule
scenes.description: Apply Bayes' rule to three real-world problems where intuition fails — medical testing, spam filtering, and tornado warnings.

views.name: Side
views.description: See the Z-layers from the side

scenes.markdown:
# Bayes' Rule

## Derivation

From the symmetry of joint probability:
$$P(A, B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

Rearranging gives **Bayes' rule**:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

where the denominator expands as the **law of total probability**:
$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{A})$$

## Interpretation

| Term | Name | Meaning |
|------|------|---------|
| $P(A)$ | **Prior** | Belief before seeing evidence |
| $P(B \mid A)$ | **Likelihood** | How probable the evidence is given A |
| $P(B)$ | **Marginal** | Total probability of the evidence |
| $P(A \mid B)$ | **Posterior** | Updated belief after seeing evidence |

## Base Rate Neglect

The most common intuition failure: ignoring the **prior** $P(A)$. When the base rate is very low, even a highly accurate test produces mostly false positives.

steps.title: Bayes' Rule
steps.description:
From the symmetry of joint probability:
$$P(A, B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

Rearranging gives **Bayes' rule**:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is the **belief update formula**: start with prior $P(A)$, observe evidence $B$, compute posterior $P(A \mid B)$. The denominator $P(B)$ just normalizes so probabilities sum to 1.

Try setting both likelihood sliders to the same value to see the independence special case.

info.content:
### Bayes' Rule
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$
---
**Prior** $P(A) = {{pA}}$
**Likelihood** $P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
**Marginal** $P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
**Posterior** $P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Independent: P(B) cancels, P(A|B) = P(A)' : pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA) > pA ? 'B raises belief in A' : 'B lowers belief in A'}}

steps.title: Example 1 — Medical Testing
steps.description:
A disease affects **1%** of people. A test is **99% sensitive** and has a **2% false positive rate**.

**You test positive. What's the probability you actually have the disease?**

Most people say ~99%. The real answer at 1% prevalence is ~**33%** — still a surprise.

**Left column** (width = prevalence) = disease. **Right column** = no disease. The **bottom row** (below the step line) = tested positive. Green = true positive, orange = false positive. The posterior $P(D \mid +)$ is the green fraction of that bottom row.

Slide prevalence down to **0.1%** to see the famous 4.7% result.

steps.title: Example 2 — Spam Filtering
steps.description:
**15%** of emails are spam. **80%** of spam contains "lottery". Only **5%** of legitimate emails contain it.

**An email with "lottery" arrives — what's the probability it's spam?**

**Left column** (width = 15%) = spam. **Right column** = legit. **Bottom row** = emails containing the keyword. Orange = spam∩word, blue = legit∩word. The posterior $P(\text{spam} \mid W) \approx 73.8\%$ is the orange fraction of the bottom row.

info.content:
### Spam Filter
Keyword: "lottery"
---
$P(\text{spam}) = {{pSpam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{spam}) = {{pWspam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{legit}) = {{pWleg}}$
---
**Per 10,000 emails:**
Spam with word: {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam*10000,0)}}
Legit with word: {{toFixed((1-pSpam)*pWleg*10000,0)}}
Total with word: {{toFixed((pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg)*10000,0)}}
---
$P(\text{spam} \mid W) = {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam/(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg)*100,1)}}\%$
---
{{pSpam*pWspam/(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg) < 0.3 ? 'Mostly legit — word is too common to filter' : pSpam*pWspam/(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg) < 0.7 ? 'Mixed signal — filter with caution' : 'Strong signal: word is a reliable spam indicator'}}

steps.title: Example 3 — Tornado Warning
steps.description:
Only **2%** of severe storm alerts are real tornadoes. Radar detects **95%** of actual tornadoes, but triggers for **3%** of non-tornado storms.

**The radar fires. What's the probability of an actual tornado?** Answer: ~**39.3%**.

**Left column** (width = 2%) = real tornadoes. **Right column** = non-tornado storms. **Bottom row** = radar fired. Red cell = true alarm, orange = false alarm. The posterior P(T|radar) is the red fraction of the bottom row.

**Try increasing the base rate**: as tornadoes become more common, the posterior climbs rapidly.

scenes.prompt:
You are teaching Bayes' rule using the correct area-based rectangle model.

## The Core Geometric Model (CRITICAL)
The rectangle divides into four cells:
- **Columns** = prior categories (disease/healthy, spam/legit, tornado/no tornado). Column WIDTH = prior probability.
- **Rows within each column** = conditional distribution P(evidence|category). Row HEIGHT in each column = P(evidence|category).
- **Cell area** = P(category) × P(evidence|category) = P(category, evidence) — joint probability.
- The stepped dividing line separates positive evidence (bottom row) from negative (top row).

## Independence Special Case
When A and B are independent, P(B|A) = P(B|¬A) = P(B). Substituting into Bayes' rule:
  P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B) = P(B)·P(A) / P(B) = P(A)
The posterior equals the prior — observing B gives zero information about A. Geometrically: the step line is horizontal, so the B-row is a rectangle and conditioning on it doesn't change the column proportions.

## For Real-World Examples
All three examples use the same column/row structure:
- Column width = base rate (P(disease), P(spam), P(tornado))
- Row height in each column = conditional test/signal probability
- The stepped dividing line size = how informative the test is
- Base rate neglect = the left column is tiny, so even a tall green cell has small area

## Exploration Prompts
- 'Set both likelihood sliders equal — the panel shows the independence collapse P(A|B) = P(A)'
- 'In the medical example, try prevalence 10% vs 0.1% and see how the posterior swings'
- 'What makes a test useful? Both low FPR and high base rate matter'
- 'Compare all three examples: which has the most dramatic base rate effect?'
